era_context: The penultimate year of Emperor Hadrian's reign (117–138 CE). Hadrian, aged 60, is still actively governing but beginning to show signs of age. In China, the Eastern Han Dynasty continues its gradual decline. The Parthian Empire under Vologases III dominates the Near East and Central Asia. This year falls within the broader era of the 'Five Good Emperors' in Rome, though Hadrian's reign is characterized more by consolidation and architectural ambition than by the peaceful stability that will define Antoninus Pius's rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most regions outside the Mediterranean and China

events.title: Hadrian continues administrative and architectural projects
events.description: Emperor Hadrian, in the second-to-last year of his reign, continues his extensive architectural and administrative projects throughout the empire. Hadrian is known for his building programs, including the Pantheon (completed c. 126 CE), the Temple of Venus and Roma, and various public works. He also continues his travels and administrative oversight of the provinces. This year represents the continuation of Hadrian's policy of consolidation and cultural patronage, which has characterized his 20-year reign.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Hadrianic building projects

events.certainty_note: Hadrian's architectural and administrative activities are well-attested for his reign generally, but specific events for 136 CE are not documented. The year is assigned based on the broader pattern of his reign.
cross_references: Part of broader Hadrianic reign (117–138 CE)

events.title: No other major documented events for this year
events.description: 136 CE is not marked by any major documented historical events in surviving sources beyond the continuation of Hadrian's administrative and architectural activities. The year falls within a period of relative stability in the Roman Empire and continued decline in the Eastern Han Dynasty, but no specific events of historical significance are recorded.
events.certainty_note: The absence of documented events is consistent with the sparse documentation of the 2nd century CE.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The sparse documentation for 136 CE reflects the general pattern of 2nd-century sources.
historiographic_note: 136 CE is poorly documented in surviving sources. The year is known primarily as part of Hadrian's reign, but specific events are not detailed in available sources. The Historia Augusta is the primary source but is known to contain fabrications and anachronisms. Archaeological evidence provides more reliable information about Hadrian's building projects, but precise dating to individual years is often difficult. Chinese sources are similarly silent on specific events for 136 CE. The year represents a period of relative stability in the Roman Empire but is not marked by any crisis, succession, or major military campaign significant enough to be recorded in detail.

graph_edges.note: Hadrian's architectural and administrative activities continue throughout his reign (117–138 CE)
Hadrian's reign and projects end with his death in 138 CE
